label: delphi-r2-p2
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 70,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "More likely than not, a reviewing court finds at least a substantial part of the surcharge non-compliant with art. XIII D, \u00a76(b) \u2014 most plausibly the portion of the $0.85/hcf figure attributable to \"conservation-incentive pricing\" beyond the documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential, and/or the revenue-use structure (rebates flowing mostly to non-payers, conservation-education staffing) that looks like cross-subsidization rather than cost-of-service recovery. Full invalidation of the entire surcharge is less likely than partial invalidation/remand for recalculation, but the proposition as framed (\"in whole or in substantial part\") is likely satisfied either way. The district's fallback \"not a property-related fee\" argument is weak and unlikely to avoid the merits.",
  "rationales": [
    "Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 holds tiered water rates satisfy \u00a76(b) only to the extent tied to actual cost of service at that tier and rejects freestanding conservation-goal pricing, and the district's own admission that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation tracks the precise defect that doomed San Juan Capistrano's rate structure.",
    "Article XIII D, \u00a76(b)(5) places the burden of proof on the agency in any legal challenge, and the district's concession that the rebate program 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' will make it difficult to prove the fee is not cross-subsidizing other customer classes in violation of \u00a76(b)(3)'s parcel-proportionality requirement.",
    "The 1.6x marginal-cost finding (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) is a genuine cost-of-service anchor that meaningfully distinguishes this case from Capistrano's total absence of a supporting cost study, making complete invalidation less likely than a court simply capping or remanding the rate to the cost-justified differential.",
    "Directing 15% of proceeds to conservation-education staffing and 55% to rebates that primarily benefit non-payers implicates \u00a76(b)(1)-(2)'s limits on using fee revenue for purposes broader than the cost of serving the paying parcels, independent of whether any portion of the rate differential itself is cost-justified.",
    "The district's alternative argument that the surcharge is not a 'property-related fee' is very likely to fail under Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205, which treats charges for metered/ongoing water service as property-related fees subject to Article XIII D, so the court will reach and likely rule against the district on the cost-of-service merits rather than dismiss on a threshold ground."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "Cal. Const. art. XIII D, \u00a76(b)(1)-(5)",
    "Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493",
    "Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205",
    "Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (verify holding re: cost-based tiered charges)",
    "Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) \u2014 verify citation and holding re: tiered/drought-surcharge cost justification",
    "Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 (procedural/validation-action implications, not merits)",
    "Cal. Const. art. XIII C, \u00a71(e) (Prop 26 tax definition and fee exceptions)",
    "Cal. Const. art. X, \u00a72 (conservation mandate)",
    "Water Code \u00a7375 (conservation program financing authority)"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "What dollar portion of the $0.85/hcf surcharge is actually attributable to the 1.6x cost differential versus pure conservation-incentive markup is not specified in the hypothetical, and this factual gap is likely dispositive of whether a court finds full compliance, partial invalidation, or total invalidation \u2014 without expert/rate-study evidence on this allocation, any probability estimate is necessarily a judgment call about how courts tend to resolve ambiguous cost-allocation records against the agency bearing the burden of proof."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 70.0, mean 71.4, std dev 2.8, range 70.0–78.0, middle 50% of panel between 70.0 and 72.0.

*(For themes below, "higher-probability panelists" refers to the two panelists at 72 and 78; "lower-probability panelists" refers to the five at 70.)*

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — The "in part conservation-incentive pricing" admission tracks the Capistrano defect (7 of 7).**
Every panelist reasoned that the district's own rate-study language — deriving $0.85 "in part" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — maps onto the defect condemned in *Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano*. Characterizations of severity varied within the theme (from "direct doctrinal vulnerability" to "close to a per se violation for the excess increment").

**Theme B — Rebate cross-subsidy as an independent revenue-use/proportionality problem (7 of 7).**
All panelists cited the concession that 55% of proceeds fund rebates mostly benefiting non-payers, invoking some combination of §6(b)(1), (b)(2), and (b)(3). Several framed this as analytically distinct from — and potentially independently dispositive of — the pricing-methodology issue.

**Theme C — The 1.6x documented cost differential as a genuine cost anchor favoring partial rather than total invalidation (7 of 7).**
Every panelist treated the peaking-capacity/drought-reserve cost study as a real cost-of-service basis that makes severance, capping, remand, or partial upholding more likely than wholesale invalidation, and distinguished this record from the agency record in *Capistrano*.

**Theme D — Agency burden of proof under §6(b)(5) (4 of 7).**
Four panelists (three at 70; the panelist at 78) reasoned that the agency's burden of proving compliance, combined with its record concessions, shifts practical litigation risk to the district. One of these grounded the burden/preponderance standard in *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir*; the others cited the constitutional text directly.

**Theme E — Bighorn forecloses the "not a property-related fee" fallback (5 of 7).**
Five panelists (all at 70) reasoned the district's threshold argument fails under *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil*, forcing the case to be decided on cost-of-service merits. The two higher-probability panelists did not include this in their rationales (one cited *Bighorn* only in authorities; one cited it for a different proposition — see disagreements).

**Theme F — Judicial deference / no mathematical precision required (3 of 7, skewing higher-probability).**
Both higher-probability panelists (72, 78) plus one panelist at 70 invoked deference authority (*Wilde*, *Griffith*, *Coziahr*) as a countervailing consideration that could salvage the full rate or the district's blended methodology.

**Theme G — 15% conservation-education staffing as a general public benefit problem (3 of 7).**
Three panelists separately flagged the "water ambassador" allocation as funding a general governmental/public-education service rather than service to the paying parcels.

**Theme H — 30% recycled-water debt service as the most defensible tranche (2 of 7).**
Two panelists (one at 70, one at 78) identified the debt-service allocation as squarely traceable to the cost rationale and likely to survive, supporting a "substantial part" rather than "whole" outcome.

**Theme I — Article X, §2 / Water Code §375 cannot override Prop 218's specific text (2 of 7, both at 70).**
Two panelists expressly reasoned that the conservation-mandate defense fails against the later-adopted, more specific §6(b) requirements.

**Cross-cutting uncertainty (flagged by all 7 in their uncertainty statements):** the unquantified gap between $0.85/hcf and what the 1.6x differential would strictly justify, which panelists uniformly identified as potentially outcome-determinative.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **Identity and holding of *Coziahr v. Otay Water District*.** Panelists cited three incompatible citations: (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 850, (2023) 91 Cal.App.5th 1, and (2024) 103 Cal.App.5th 122. One panelist relied on it affirmatively as deference-to-methodology authority; three others flagged its holding as unverified, and one was uncertain whether it narrows or reinforces *Capistrano*. The panel does not agree on what this case is or says.

2. **How strict *Capistrano* is versus the deference line of cases.** Some panelists treat the "in part incentive-pricing" admission as effectively fatal to the increment above cost (approaching a per se defect); others posit that *Wilde*/*Griffith*-style latitude for non-precise cost allocation could sustain the full $0.85 as a reasonable cost-based approximation. One panelist explicitly framed this as an unresolved doctrinal tension with no controlling Supreme Court decision.

3. **Which *Griffith* case supplies the countervailing authority.** One panelist relied on *Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz* (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130 as tolerating conservation-oriented tiered pricing; four others cited *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586. These are different cases with different holdings being used for a similar purpose.

4. **What *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* stands for.** One panelist cited it for the agency's burden of proof by a preponderance; another cited it for rate-setting deference and the absence of a mathematical-precision requirement. These are different readings of the same authority.

5. **Whether the rebate program is defensible at all.** Most panelists treat the cross-subsidy concession as essentially indefensible under §6(b)(2)–(3). One panelist (78) identified a specific path by which the district could prevail on it — that rebates reduce system-wide peaking/drought-reserve costs and thereby benefit payors — while concluding the concessions make that a "harder sell."

6. **Whether any partial invalidation necessarily satisfies "in substantial part."** Most panelists assume partial invalidation satisfies the proposition as framed. One panelist (70) expressly disagreed with that assumption, reasoning that a "minor trim" to the rate might arguably not be "substantial," making the unquantified dollar magnitude the crux of whether the proposition is true.

7. **What *Bighorn* is authority for.** Most panelists cited *Bighorn* solely for the property-related-fee threshold; one panelist also cited it (with *Richmond v. Shasta*) as informing the substantive cross-subsidization/proportionality analysis.

## 4. Authorities

*Note: one panelist (at 70) submitted no formal authorities list, though that panelist's rationales expressly referenced Capistrano, Bighorn-Desert, and art. X, §2. Counts below reflect formal authorities lists; parentheticals note that panelist's in-rationale references where applicable.*

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)(1)–(5) — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- *Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano* (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil* (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference; one panelist spelled "Vejil")
- Cal. Const. art. XIII C / §1(e) (Prop 26) — 6
- Cal. Const. art. X, §2 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- Water Code §375 — 6
- *Coziahr v. Otay Water District* — 5 (with divergent years and citations; see Disagreement #1; four of five flagged "verify")
- *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 — 4 (two flagged verification of pinpoint applicability)
- *Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District* (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 — 3
- *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — 2

**Cited by only one panelist:**
- *Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz* (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130
- *City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist.* (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926
- *Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist.* (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409
- *Zolly v. City of Oakland* (2022) 13 Cal.5th 858 (cited with a "verify relevance" flag)
- Gov. Code §66022

---
*Round 2 instructions: Review the themes, disagreements, and authorities above. You may revise or maintain your probability and rationales in light of what other panelists raised.*

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":73,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Revising slightly upward from 70 to 73. The panel's near-unanimous identification of the rebate cross-subsidy (Theme B) as an independently dispositive defect under §6(b)(1)-(3) — analytically distinct from the pricing-methodology defect (Theme A) — persuades me the proposition has two largely independent paths to being satisfied, which raises the disjunctive probability above where I'd land assessing either defect alone. Combined with the §6(b)(5) burden-of-proof point (Theme D), which the district's own record concessions make hard to carry, I think 'substantial part' invalidation is somewhat more likely than my round-1 estimate suggested. I did not move to the panel's upper end (78) because the deference countervailing line (Theme F) and the genuinely unresolved Coziahr/Griffith citation confusion (Disagreements #1, #3) represent real doctrinal uncertainty I won't discount to zero, and Disagreement #6 (whether a 'minor trim' counts as 'substantial') is a legitimate basis for retaining meaningful probability mass on partial, non-substantial outcomes.","rationales":["The rebate cross-subsidy (55% of revenue funding rebates the district concedes 'mostly benefit customers other than those paying the surcharge') is doctrinally severable from the pricing-methodology defect: even if a court accepted the full $0.85/hcf as a reasonable cost-based rate, the revenue-use structure independently implicates §6(b)(1)-(2)'s cap on proceeds exceeding funds required to serve the fee payer and §6(b)(3)'s parcel-proportionality requirement. Two largely independent theories of invalidation increase the probability at least one succeeds under the proposition's disjunctive 'whole or substantial part' framing — this is the main thing that moved me.","Article XIII D, §6(b)(5) places the burden of proving compliance on the district, and its own rate-study language ('in part' conservation-incentive pricing) and litigation concessions (rebates flow mostly to non-payers) function as admissions against interest that will be difficult to explain away at trial, shifting practical risk toward invalidation of at least a portion of the structure.","The genuine cost anchor (1.6x marginal-cost differential for peaking/drought-reserve capacity) and the plausibly defensible 30% debt-service tranche (Theme H) still make total invalidation of the entire surcharge unlikely; the more probable outcome is a court severing/capping the increment above documented cost and/or invalidating the rebate-funded tranche while leaving the debt-service tranche intact — but this outcome still satisfies the proposition as framed ('in whole or in substantial part'), which is why it doesn't pull my number down further.","The countervailing deference line (Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir, Griffith, and the contested Coziahr citation) genuinely could sustain a blended cost/incentive methodology as a 'reasonable approximation' rather than requiring precise proportionality, and one panelist's point that rebates arguably reduce system-wide peaking costs (thus indirectly benefiting payors) is a colorable, if 'harder sell,' defense — this is real uncertainty that keeps me from moving toward the panel's 78 ceiling.","The unresolved identity of Coziahr v. Otay Water District (three incompatible citations across the panel) and the conflicting Griffith cases (Pajaro Valley vs. City of Santa Cruz) mean a key piece of the deference-side authority is unverified and could cut either way once confirmed, which is an independent reason to keep confidence at 'medium' rather than 'high' despite the upward point-estimate revision."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)(1)-(5)","Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 (verify whether cited for burden of proof or rate-setting deference/no-mathematical-precision)","Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (verify applicability; distinguish from Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130)","Coziahr v. Otay Water District — citation and holding unverified/disputed across panel (2018 24 Cal.App.5th 850 vs. 2023 91 Cal.App.5th 1 vs. 2024 103 Cal.App.5th 122)","Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 (procedural, not merits)","Cal. Const. art. XIII C, §1(e) (Prop 26)","Cal. Const. art. X, §2; Water Code §375","Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist. (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409 (verify relevance to cross-subsidy analysis)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Two compounding unknowns: (1) the unquantified dollar gap between $0.85/hcf and what the 1.6x cost differential would strictly justify, which determines whether the pricing defect is minor or severe; and (2) the actual holding of Coziahr v. Otay Water District, which the panel could not agree on (three different citations proposed) and which could function as either a significant deference anchor for the district or a reinforcement of Capistrano's strictness — without resolving what this case actually says, I can't calibrate how much weight the 'deference' countervailing theme should carry against the two independent invalidation theories.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"Theme B (rebate cross-subsidy as independently dispositive, 7/7) is what actually moved my number — I had listed the rebate issue in round 1 but treated it as reinforcing the same underlying defect as the pricing-methodology problem rather than as a doctrinally separate path to invalidation under §6(b)(1)-(2) that operates even if the $0.85 rate itself were upheld as cost-justified. Recognizing it as a second, largely independent theory pushes the disjunctive probability up. Theme D (burden of proof) reinforced this modestly. I deliberately did not follow the panel toward 78: Theme F (deference doctrine) and the disagreements over Coziahr/Griffith's actual holdings are real unresolved authority questions, not just noise, and I think they justify keeping meaningful probability mass on a 'partial, non-substantial trim' outcome (Disagreement #6) that would falsify the proposition as framed. Net effect: modest upward revision to 73, not convergence to the panel ceiling."}

data.position_summary: Revising slightly upward from 70 to 73. The panel's near-unanimous identification of the rebate cross-subsidy (Theme B) as an independently dispositive defect under §6(b)(1)-(3) — analytically distinct from the pricing-methodology defect (Theme A) — persuades me the proposition has two largely independent paths to being satisfied, which raises the disjunctive probability above where I'd land assessing either defect alone. Combined with the §6(b)(5) burden-of-proof point (Theme D), which the district's own record concessions make hard to carry, I think 'substantial part' invalidation is somewhat more likely than my round-1 estimate suggested. I did not move to the panel's upper end (78) because the deference countervailing line (Theme F) and the genuinely unresolved Coziahr/Griffith citation confusion (Disagreements #1, #3) represent real doctrinal uncertainty I won't discount to zero, and Disagreement #6 (whether a 'minor trim' counts as 'substantial') is a legitimate basis for retaining meaningful probability mass on partial, non-substantial outcomes.
rationales: The rebate cross-subsidy (55% of revenue funding rebates the district concedes 'mostly benefit customers other than those paying the surcharge') is doctrinally severable from the pricing-methodology defect: even if a court accepted the full $0.85/hcf as a reasonable cost-based rate, the revenue-use structure independently implicates §6(b)(1)-(2)'s cap on proceeds exceeding funds required to serve the fee payer and §6(b)(3)'s parcel-proportionality requirement. Two largely independent theories of invalidation increase the probability at least one succeeds under the proposition's disjunctive 'whole or substantial part' framing — this is the main thing that moved me.

Article XIII D, §6(b)(5) places the burden of proving compliance on the district, and its own rate-study language ('in part' conservation-incentive pricing) and litigation concessions (rebates flow mostly to non-payers) function as admissions against interest that will be difficult to explain away at trial, shifting practical risk toward invalidation of at least a portion of the structure.

The genuine cost anchor (1.6x marginal-cost differential for peaking/drought-reserve capacity) and the plausibly defensible 30% debt-service tranche (Theme H) still make total invalidation of the entire surcharge unlikely; the more probable outcome is a court severing/capping the increment above documented cost and/or invalidating the rebate-funded tranche while leaving the debt-service tranche intact — but this outcome still satisfies the proposition as framed ('in whole or in substantial part'), which is why it doesn't pull my number down further.

The countervailing deference line (Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir, Griffith, and the contested Coziahr citation) genuinely could sustain a blended cost/incentive methodology as a 'reasonable approximation' rather than requiring precise proportionality, and one panelist's point that rebates arguably reduce system-wide peaking costs (thus indirectly benefiting payors) is a colorable, if 'harder sell,' defense — this is real uncertainty that keeps me from moving toward the panel's 78 ceiling.

The unresolved identity of Coziahr v. Otay Water District (three incompatible citations across the panel) and the conflicting Griffith cases (Pajaro Valley vs. City of Santa Cruz) mean a key piece of the deference-side authority is unverified and could cut either way once confirmed, which is an independent reason to keep confidence at 'medium' rather than 'high' despite the upward point-estimate revision.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 (verify whether cited for burden of proof or rate-setting deference/no-mathematical-precision)
Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (verify applicability; distinguish from Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130)
Coziahr v. Otay Water District — citation and holding unverified/disputed across panel (2018 24 Cal.App.5th 850 vs. 2023 91 Cal.App.5th 1 vs. 2024 103 Cal.App.5th 122)
Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 (procedural, not merits)
Cal. Const. art. X, §2; Water Code §375
Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist. (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409 (verify relevance to cross-subsidy analysis)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Two compounding unknowns: (1) the unquantified dollar gap between $0.85/hcf and what the 1.6x cost differential would strictly justify, which determines whether the pricing defect is minor or severe; and (2) the actual holding of Coziahr v. Otay Water District, which the panel could not agree on (three different citations proposed) and which could function as either a significant deference anchor for the district or a reinforcement of Capistrano's strictness — without resolving what this case actually says, I can't calibrate how much weight the 'deference' countervailing theme should carry against the two independent invalidation theories.
data.response_to_panel: Theme B (rebate cross-subsidy as independently dispositive, 7/7) is what actually moved my number — I had listed the rebate issue in round 1 but treated it as reinforcing the same underlying defect as the pricing-methodology problem rather than as a doctrinally separate path to invalidation under §6(b)(1)-(2) that operates even if the $0.85 rate itself were upheld as cost-justified. Recognizing it as a second, largely independent theory pushes the disjunctive probability up. Theme D (burden of proof) reinforced this modestly. I deliberately did not follow the panel toward 78: Theme F (deference doctrine) and the disagreements over Coziahr/Griffith's actual holdings are real unresolved authority questions, not just noise, and I think they justify keeping meaningful probability mass on a 'partial, non-substantial trim' outcome (Disagreement #6) that would falsify the proposition as framed. Net effect: modest upward revision to 73, not convergence to the panel ceiling.
